label: vulnerable

source.description: User-controlled string injected via @RequestParam 'filename'. Spring binds the raw query parameter value without any sanitization.
source.code_snippet: public byte[] downloadFile(@RequestParam String filename) throws IOException {

propagation.description: Paths.get(BASE_DIR, filename) appends the user-supplied string to the base directory. Unlike Path.resolve(), Paths.get() does not normalize '..' components; they are preserved literally in the resulting path.

sink.description: Files.readAllBytes() reads the file at the unvalidated path. An attacker can supply '../../etc/passwd' to read arbitrary files outside BASE_DIR.

expected_findings.risk_reasoning: The filename parameter flows directly from the HTTP request into Paths.get() and then Files.readAllBytes() without normalization or prefix validation. '..' traversal sequences are not stripped, enabling arbitrary file read.
expected_findings.remediation_hint: Replace the raw path construction with: Path resolved = BASE_DIR.resolve(filename).normalize(); then assert resolved.toRealPath().startsWith(BASE_DIR.toRealPath()) before reading.

recall_anchor.description: A correct system must identify the sink region where the unnormalized path enters Files.readAllBytes().

notes: Paths.get(base, filename) differs from Path.resolve(filename): both preserve '..' but the distinction from Python's os.path.join is worth noting — Java's Paths.get with multiple components does not normalize.
